claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 9 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 5 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 9 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 5 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 9 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 5 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To get started, I need to ask you some questions about your child.

**First, how old is your child?** 

This is important because:
- Children **under 16** generally qualify automatically (if not working full-time)
- Children **aged 16-19** may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- Children **aged 20 or over** do not qualify for Child Benefit

Please tell me your child's age, and if you have more than one child, we can go through them one at a time.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, thanks for helping me with this. I have two children actually - Alex who's 9 years old, and Blake who's 5. They both live with me at home.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! It's great that you have two children, Alex (9 years old) and Blake (5 years old), both living with you at home.

Since both of your children are under 16, let me continue with the assessment. Let me check the next question for children in this age group.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Now, the next question:

**Are your children working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work at all)?**

Since Alex is 9 and Blake is 5, I imagine they're not in paid employment, but I need to confirm this to continue with the assessment. This rule mainly applies to older children, but I need to verify it for the eligibility check.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Oh no, they're not working at all - they're just kids! Alex is in primary school and Blake is still quite little, so no jobs for either of them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's exactly what I expected! Thank you for confirming. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Next question:

**Are your children NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right** (such as Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit)?

Again, since Alex and Blake are only 9 and 5 years old, they almost certainly wouldn't be claiming benefits in their own right, but I need to confirm this for the assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them get any benefits in their own names. They're too young for anything like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, that makes sense. Let me continue to the next step.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Next question:

**Are your children in any type of apprenticeship program?**

This is relevant for older children (typically 16+), but again I need to verify. Since Alex is 9 and Blake is 5, I assume they're not in any apprenticeship, but please confirm.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, definitely not - they're way too young for apprenticeships. Alex is just in primary school and Blake is only 5!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Absolutely right! Let me move forward with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now we need to check your residency status. 

**Do you meet UK residency requirements?**

You would qualify if **ANY** of the following applies to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK**
2. You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have pre-settled status and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months)
4. You have pre-settled status and are actively seeking work (up to 91 days)
5. You have pre-settled status with sufficient resources to support yourself and your family
6. You have pre-settled status as a student with sufficient resources
7. You have pre-settled status as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national with right to reside
8. You're living abroad as a Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

**Do any of these apply to you?** Most people will simply answer "yes" if they normally live in the UK.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester with the kids.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! You meet the residency requirement as you normally live in the UK. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Now I need to check about your responsibility for Alex and Blake.

**Are you responsible for your children?**

You would qualify if **ANY** of the following applies:

1. **You live with the child** (which you've already mentioned you do!)
2. You're contributing to the child's upkeep by paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards their care (food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents, etc.)
3. You're paying maintenance under a court order or agreement that equals or exceeds the Child Benefit payment

Since you mentioned that both Alex and Blake live with you at home in Manchester, it sounds like you meet the responsibility requirement. Can you confirm that?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - they both live with me at home, so I'm responsible for them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue with the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to ask an important question:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex or Blake?**

For example:
- Another parent or guardian
- A former partner
- Another family member

This is important because only one person can claim Child Benefit for each child at a time. If someone else is claiming, you would need to agree with them about transferring the claim to you.

What's the situation with your children?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for them. It's just me looking after them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now I need to check about your children's living situation:

**Are either Alex or Blake currently:**
- In hospital?
- In local authority care (placed by the council)?
- Fostered?
- Or are they living normally at home with you?

Please let me know which applies to your situation.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: They're just living normally at home with me - nothing like hospital or care or anything like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Just one more question:

**Is the local council NOT paying towards Alex or Blake's accommodation or maintenance?**

In other words, are you paying for their living costs yourself (rather than the council paying for them)? Since they're living at home with you, I expect the answer is that the council is NOT paying for them, but I need to confirm this.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right - the council isn't paying anything. I pay for everything myself for both of them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent news! Based on all the information you've provided, let me move to the final step.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## 🎉 Great News - You ARE Eligible for Child Benefit!

**You meet all the eligibility requirements for Child Benefit for both Alex and Blake.**

### Why you're eligible:

**For Alex (9 years old):**
- Under 16 years old ✓
- Not in paid work ✓
- Not receiving benefits in their own right ✓
- Lives with you at home ✓
- You're responsible for them ✓
- No one else is claiming for them ✓
- Council is not paying for their care ✓

**For Blake (5 years old):**
- Under 16 years old ✓
- Not in paid work ✓
- Not receiving benefits in their own right ✓
- Lives with you at home ✓
- You're responsible for them ✓
- No one else is claiming for them ✓
- Council is not paying for their care ✓

**You also meet the requirements:**
- You normally live in the UK (Manchester) ✓

### Next Steps to Claim:

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and both children
3. **Provide birth certificates** (or adoption certificates) for Alex and Blake
4. **Be aware**: If you or your partner earns over £60,000, you may need to pay the High Income Child Benefit Charge (though you can still claim)
5. **Payments**: Typically made every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

### More Information:
You can apply and find more details at: **https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim**

Do you have any questions about applying or anything else related to Child Benefit?

reasonings: Alex is 9 years old (under 16), not in paid work, not receiving benefits in their own right, not in an apprenticeship, and lives with the claimant who is responsible for them. The claimant meets UK residency requirements (lives in Manchester), no one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, Alex is living normally at home, and the local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.

Blake is 5 years old (under 16), not in paid work, not receiving benefits in their own right, not in an apprenticeship, and lives with the claimant who is responsible for them. The claimant meets UK residency requirements (lives in Manchester), no one else is claiming Child Benefit for Blake, Blake is living normally at home, and the local council is not paying towards Blake's accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has two children (Alex, 9, and Blake, 5) both living at home in Manchester. The claimant meets all UK residency requirements, is responsible for both children, no other person is claiming Child Benefit for either child, both children are living normally at home, and the council is not paying towards their care. Both children are under 16 and not in any disqualifying circumstances. The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for both children.

response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances
